cases.title: Collapse in a Shopping Mall
cases.presentation: You are a BLS provider walking through a shopping mall when a man in his 60s suddenly collapses. He is unresponsive and not breathing normally. A crowd gathers, and you notice an AED cabinet on the wall about 15 meters away. Two bystanders are nearby.
cases.question: Applying the Chain of Survival, what is the most appropriate immediate set of actions?
options: A: Direct a bystander to call emergency services and another to bring the AED, and begin high-quality chest compressions now.
B: Wait for EMS to arrive before touching the patient.
C: Perform a detailed head-to-toe assessment before starting CPR.
D: Retrieve the AED yourself first, leaving the patient, before starting any compressions.
cases.explanation: The first links of the chain are activation and high-quality CPR. Delegating the call and AED retrieval to bystanders lets you start compressions immediately without leaving the patient, compressing the time to both CPR and defibrillation.
cases.clinicalPearl: Delegate clearly and specifically ('You, call 911; you, bring that AED') — vague requests to a crowd often go unanswered.

cases.title: Ward Patient Deteriorating
cases.presentation: On a hospital ward, a monitored patient's early-warning score rises sharply over 20 minutes: increasing respiratory rate, falling blood pressure, and new confusion. The patient still has a pulse and is breathing.
cases.question: Which system-of-care action best reflects the in-hospital emphasis of the Chain of Survival here?
options: A: Do nothing until the patient arrests, then start CPR.
B: Activate the rapid-response/medical emergency team to intervene before arrest occurs.
C: Apply an AED to the conscious patient with a pulse.
cases.explanation: In-hospital care emphasizes surveillance and prevention. Activating the rapid-response team addresses deterioration early and may prevent the arrest entirely — compressions and AED are not indicated in a patient who has a pulse and is breathing.
cases.clinicalPearl: The best cardiac arrest is the one that never happens — early-warning systems and rapid-response teams are the prevention link in action.

cases.title: After the Pulse Returns
cases.presentation: Your team resuscitated a woman in cardiac arrest at home. After several cycles of CPR and one AED shock, she regains a pulse and begins breathing on her own. The family, relieved, asks if everything is 'over now.'
cases.question: Based on the Chain of Survival, what is the most appropriate next step and message?
options: A: Tell the family it is over and stop monitoring the patient.
B: Continue close monitoring, support post-arrest care and transport, and explain that recovery is an ongoing process.
C: Remove the AED pads and leave the scene.
D: Discharge the patient at home since she has a pulse.
cases.explanation: ROSC is not the end. Post-cardiac arrest care (link 5) and recovery (link 6) continue: the patient needs monitoring, transport, and ongoing care, and the family should understand that recovery is a journey requiring further treatment and support.
cases.clinicalPearl: Re-arrest is common early after ROSC — keep the AED attached and reassess frequently during transport.
